zones.label: Internal Flash
zones.summary: Non-volatile program storage built into the board
facts: Size (Pico): 2 MB QSPI NOR flash (Pico 2: 4 MB)
Volatile: No — data survives power-off and resets
Read speed: ~400 kB/s via XIP (execute in place; cached for code)
Write speed: ~100 kB/s in 4 KB erase sectors; ~100,000 write cycles
File system: LittleFS (default MicroPython) or FAT
Used for: .py source, compiled .mpy bytecode, boot.py, main.py
Accessed via: os.listdir('/'); open('/myfile.txt', 'w')
Cost: included in board price
Best for: storing your program, config files, small data logs

zones.label: SRAM (Internal RAM)
zones.summary: Fast volatile working memory for running code
facts: Size (Pico): 264 KB total SRAM — 6 banks on RP2040
Volatile: Yes — all data lost on reset or power-off
Read/write speed: ~133 MHz access (single-cycle via bus fabric)
MicroPython heap: ~170–200 KB available to Python objects
File system: N/A — RAM is not a file system
Used for: variables, lists, dicts, bytearray and network buffers
Accessed via: normal Python variables
Monitor: micropython.mem_info() shows free heap
Best for: all runtime data — free memory is the #1 constraint

zones.label: microSD Card (SPI)
zones.summary: Large external storage read over the SPI bus
facts: Capacity: 1 GB–32 GB (FAT32); up to 2 TB (exFAT)
Volatile: No — data survives power-off
Read speed: 0.5–4 MB/s via SPI (less than native SDIO)
Write speed: 0.3–2 MB/s via SPI
File system: FAT16 / FAT32 / exFAT (works with PCs)
Used for: CSV logs, audio samples, large datasets, images
Accessed via: sdcard.py driver; mounts as /sd
Cost: $1–$5 card + $1–$3 SPI breakout
Best for: long-term logging, audio storage, moving data to a PC

zones.label: I2C EEPROM
facts: Capacity: 4 KB (AT24C32) · 32 KB (AT24C256) · 512 KB (AT24CM02)
Volatile: No — retains data for 100+ years (spec)
Read speed: ~100 kbps (I2C standard mode)
Write speed: ~100 kbps; 5 ms write cycle per page (8–64 bytes)
Write endurance: up to 1,000,000 write cycles per byte address
Used for: device config, calibration constants, serial numbers, counters
Accessed via: i2c.writeto_mem(addr, reg, data); i2c.readfrom_mem(...)
Cost: $0.50–$2.00 (breakout board)
Best for: small settings that must survive power loss

quiz.question: Which memory loses all of its data the moment the board loses power?
quiz.explanation: SRAM is volatile — variables, lists, and buffers all live here while the program runs, but everything disappears on reset or power-off. The other three keep their data without power.

quiz.question: Where do your .py files, boot.py, and compiled .mpy bytecode actually live?
quiz.explanation: Internal flash is the non-volatile program storage built into the board. MicroPython stores your source files and compiled bytecode here using the LittleFS file system.

quiz.question: Which storage holds gigabytes and uses a standard FAT file system you can read on a PC?
quiz.explanation: A microSD card connected over SPI offers 1 GB to 2 TB of FAT16/FAT32/exFAT storage — ideal for big CSV data logs and audio files you also want to open on a computer.

quiz.question: You need to save a few calibration constants that survive power loss and update them up to a million times. Which chip fits best?
quiz.explanation: An I2C EEPROM is byte-addressable, keeps data for 100+ years, and tolerates up to 1,000,000 write cycles per address — perfect for small settings and counters that must survive a reboot.

quiz.question: When you hit a MemoryError, which limited resource have you run out of?
quiz.explanation: MemoryError means the MicroPython heap in SRAM is full. Only about 170–200 KB of the 264 KB SRAM is free for Python objects, so runtime memory is the #1 constraint on embedded Python.
